  Searched for 1 pattern (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ CSS looks fine. The grid should render. The page accessibility tree showed all the elements loading correctly.
  Can you describe what you see? Is it just the palette items but no grid cells, or is the page completely blank?

  ---
  For your WebSocket server question: server.js needs a persistent Node process, which Vercel doesn't support
  (it's serverless/static only). Options:

  1. Railway — easiest. railway init → railway up, it runs node server.js on a persistent dyno. Free tier
  available.
  2. Render — free Web Service tier, set start command to node server.js
  3. Fly.io — fly launch, runs the server in a container

  Then update the WS URL in portal.html and portal.ts to point to the deployed server instead of localhost:3001.
  Want me to set one of these up?

❯ how do we deploy the websocket server separately

  Searched for 1 pattern (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ CSS looks fine. The grid should render. The page accessibility tree showed all the elements loading correctly.
  Can you describe what you see? Is it just the palette items but no grid cells, or is the page completely blank?

  ---
  For your WebSocket server question: server.js needs a persistent Node process, which Vercel doesn't support
  (it's serverless/static only). Options:

  1. Railway — easiest. railway init → railway up, it runs node server.js on a persistent dyno. Free tier
  available.
  2. Render — free Web Service tier, set start command to node server.js
  3. Fly.io — fly launch, runs the server in a container

  Then update the WS URL in portal.html and portal.ts to point to the deployed server instead of localhost:3001.
  Want me to set one of these up?

⏺ CSS looks fine. The grid should render. The page accessibility tree showed all the elements loading correctly.
  Can you describe what you see? Is it just the palette items but no grid cells, or is the page completely blank?

  ---
  For your WebSocket server question: server.js needs a persistent Node process, which Vercel doesn't support
  (it's serverless/static only). Options:

  1. Railway — easiest. railway init → railway up, it runs node server.js on a persistent dyno. Free tier
  available.
  2. Render — free Web Service tier, set start command to node server.js
  3. Fly.io — fly launch, runs the server in a container

  Then update the WS URL in portal.html and portal.ts to point to the deployed server instead of localhost:3001.
  Want me to set one of these up?

  Can you describe what you see? Is it just the palette items but no grid cells, or is the page completely blank?

  ---
  For your WebSocket server question: server.js needs a persistent Node process, which Vercel doesn't support
  (it's serverless/static only). Options:

  1. Railway — easiest. railway init → railway up, it runs node server.js on a persistent dyno. Free tier
  available.
  2. Render — free Web Service tier, set start command to node server.js
  3. Fly.io — fly launch, runs the server in a container

  Then update the WS URL in portal.html and portal.ts to point to the deployed server instead of localhost:3001.
  Want me to set one of these up?

⏺ Write(nixpacks.toml)
  ⎿  Added 7 lines, removed 1 line
       1 -[phases.build]
       1 +[phases.setup]
       2 +nixPkgs = ["nodejs_22"]
       3 +
       4 +[phases.install]
       5  cmd = "npm install"
       6
       7 +[phases.build]
       8 +cmd = "echo 'skip build'"
       9 +
      10  [start]
      11  cmd = "node server.js"

      2 WORKDIR /app
      3 COPY package*.json ./

      5 COPY server.js ./
      6 EXPOSE 3001
      7 CMD ["node", "server.js"]

⏺ I can see the problem — Caddy is serving HTTP responses (status 200, returning index.html) instead of upgrading
   WebSocket connections. The nixpacks.toml start command override isn't working because Railway still
  auto-detects and builds with Caddy.

  The fix: override the nixpacks provider to force Node.js instead of static site detection:

⏺ Write(nixpacks.toml)
  ⎿  Added 7 lines, removed 1 line
       1 -[phases.build]
       1 +[phases.setup]
       2 +nixPkgs = ["nodejs_22"]
       3 +
       4 +[phases.install]
       5  cmd = "npm install"
       6
       7 +[phases.build]
       8 +cmd = "echo 'skip build'"
       9 +
      10  [start]
      11  cmd = "node server.js"

⏺ Update(Dockerfile)

      3  COPY package*.json ./
      4 -RUN npm install --production

      6  EXPOSE 3001
      7  CMD ["node", "server.js"]

❯ /model
  ⎿  Set model to Opus 4.6 (1M context) (default)

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is it necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items from the local grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in the portal's local state and sends a GRID_PLACE message for
  each to the server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is already auto-sent via wsSend({ type: 'GRID_PLACE', ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and broadcasts updates to all clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its in-memory state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal items were already placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button — a safety net, not part of the core flow. You could remove it
   without breaking anything.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

❯ /model
  ⎿  Set model to Opus 4.6 (1M context) (default)

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is it necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items from the local grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in theportal's local state and sends a
  GRID_PLACE message for each to the server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is alreadauto-sent via wsSend({ type:
  'GRID_PLACE', ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and broadcasts updates to all clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its in-memory state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal items were already placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button — asafety net, not part of the core flow.
  You could remove it without breaking anything.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

❯ /model
  ⎿  Set model to Opus 4.6 (1M context) (default)

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is it
  necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to
  expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items from
  the local grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in the
  portal's local state and sends a GRID_PLACE message
  for each to the server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is already
   auto-sent via wsSend({ type: 'GRID_PLACE', ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and broadcasts
  updates to all clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its
  in-memory state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal items
  were already placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button — a
  safety net, not part of the core flow. You could
  remove it without breaking anything.

⏺ Actually, the real problem might be that
  Railway's nixpacks is taking priority over the
  Dockerfile. Let me check if there's a way to
  force Docker in the config:

⏺ Write(nixpacks.toml)
  ⎿  Added 1 line, removed 11 lines
       1 -[phases.setup]
       2 -nixPkgs = ["nodejs_22"]
       3 -
       4 -[phases.install]
       5 -cmd = "npm install"
       6 -
       7 -[phases.build]
       8 -cmd = "echo 'skip build'"
       9 -
      10 -[start]
      11 -cmd = "node server.js"
       1 +# Force Railway to use Dockerfile
         +instead of nixpacks auto-detection

❯ /model
  ⎿  Set model to Opus 4.6 (1M context) (default)

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is it
   necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to
  expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items
  from the local grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in
  the portal's local state and sends a GRID_PLACE
  message for each to the server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is
  already auto-sent via wsSend({ type:
  'GRID_PLACE', ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and broadcasts
   updates to all clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its
  in-memory state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal
  items were already placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button
  — a safety net, not part of the core flow. You
  could remove it without breaking anything.

❯ /model
  ⎿  Set model to Opus 4.6 (1M context) (default)

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is
  it necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to
  expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items
  from the local grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in
   the portal's local state and sends a GRID_PLACE
   message for each to the server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is
  already auto-sent via wsSend({ type:
  'GRID_PLACE', ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and
  broadcasts updates to all clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its
  in-memory state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal
  items were already placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button
   — a safety net, not part of the core flow. You
  could remove it without breaking anything.

❯ /model
  ⎿  Set model to Opus 4.6 (1M context) (default)

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is it
  necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to
  expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items from
  the local grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in
  the portal's local state and sends a GRID_PLACE
  message for each to the server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is
  already auto-sent via wsSend({ type: 'GRID_PLACE',
  ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and broadcasts
  updates to all clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its
  in-memory state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal items
   were already placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button —
  a safety net, not part of the core flow. You could
  remove it without breaking anything.

❯ /model
  ⎿  Set model to Opus 4.6 (1M context) (default)

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is it
  necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items from the
   local grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in the
  portal's local state and sends a GRID_PLACE message
  for each to the server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is already
  auto-sent via wsSend({ type: 'GRID_PLACE', ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and broadcasts
  updates to all clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its
  in-memory state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal items
  were already placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button — a
  safety net, not part of the core flow. You could
  remove it without breaking anything.

❯ /model
  ⎿  Set model to Opus 4.6 (1M context) (default)

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is it
  necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items from the
  local grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in the
  portal's local state and sends a GRID_PLACE message for
  each to the server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is already
  auto-sent via wsSend({ type: 'GRID_PLACE', ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and broadcasts updates
   to all clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its in-memory
  state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal items were
  already placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button — a
  safety net, not part of the core flow. You could remove
  it without breaking anything.

❯ /model
  ⎿  Set model to Opus 4.6 (1M context) (default)

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is it necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items from the local
   grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in the
  portal's local state and sends a GRID_PLACE message for each
   to the server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is already
  auto-sent via wsSend({ type: 'GRID_PLACE', ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and broadcasts updates to
   all clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its in-memory
  state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal items were
  already placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button — a safety
  net, not part of the core flow. You could remove it without
  breaking anything.

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is it necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items from the local
  grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in the portal's
   local state and sends a GRID_PLACE message for each to the
  server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is already
  auto-sent via wsSend({ type: 'GRID_PLACE', ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and broadcasts updates to
  all clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its in-memory
  state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal items were
  already placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button — a safety
  net, not part of the core flow. You could remove it without
  breaking anything.

❯ ok, we don't need that button, we can change the botton fso
  it is now called start round. the way it works, is in the
  center top of the interface there will be a timer. you can
  also set the time for the round at the top. when you press
  start round, it will start the round for the vr contestant.
  when the round starts, some items will come to life, some
  items will be goals to win the round. we will design what
  the emoji's do in terms of items and what not. add collision
   detection with teh user and the emoji, then we can set this
   up to be item collecting, or bad guy collisions later, or
  collecting objectives. i also want you to make the left hand
   joystic a sword, and the right one a gun. come up with this
   plan.

⏺ Let me load IWSDK planning guidance first, then draft the
  plan.

⏺ Skill(iwsdk-planner)
  ⎿  Successfully loaded skill

⏺ Round-Based Game Plan

  Phase 1 — Portal UI (round controls)

  Replace Transmit button + add timer controls in top bar:

  [Clear] [30s ▾] [00:30]← center, monospace] [Start Round]

  - <input type="number"> for duration (default 30s, 5–600)
  - <div id="timer"> — big monospace clock in center
  - <button id="start-round-btn"> — sends ROUND_START with
  duration, then locally runs countdown synced to server

  New WS message types:
  - ROUND_START: { type, duration, startedAt } — broadcast to
  all clients
  - ROUND_END: { type, reason: 'timeout'|'completed' }
  - ROUND_SCORE: { type, userId, points, reason }

  Phase 2 — Item schema & roles

  Extend grid item to include a role field:

  { type, icon, label, role }

  ┌──────────┬──────────┬──────────────────────────────────┐
  │   Role   │  Emojis  │    Behavior when round active    │
  ├──────────┼──────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
  │ goal     │ ⭐ 💎 🎯 │ Collect by touch → score +1,     │
  │          │  🌀      │ despawn, play chime              │
  ├──────────┼──────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
  │ enemy    │ 🔥 🤖 👻 │ Chase/patrol; damage player on   │
  │          │  💀      │ touch; killable with sword/gun   │
  ├──────────┼──────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
  │ pickup   │ ❤️  🍄 ⚡ │ Powerup (heal/speed) — stretch   │
  │          │          │ goal                             │
  ├──────────┼──────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
  │ obstacle │ 🪵 🧱 🪑 │ Static, impassable (later:       │
  │          │          │ physics collision)               │
  ├──────────┼──────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
  │ decor    │ 🌱 🎵 🖥️  │ Neutral, passable, no            │
  │          │  🏮      │ interaction                      │
  └──────────┴──────────┴──────────────────────────────────┘

  Portal palette gets role tags via CSS — goals glow gold,
  enemies red. No UI changes needed to place them; role is
  derived from icon.

  Phase 3 — VR round state (new ECS components/systems)

  New components:
  GameItem { role, health, points, speed, behavior }
  RoundState  // tag on singleton entity
  Weapon { type: 'sword' | 'gun', damage, cooldown }
  Projectile { velocity, lifetime, damage, ownerEntity }
  Health { current, max }

  World globals (shared state):
  world.globals.roundRunning = signal(false)
  world.globals.roundEndsAt = signal(0)
  world.globals.score = signal(0)
  world.globals.playerHealth = signal(100)

  New systems (register in order):

  ┌─────────────────┬──────────┬────────────────────────────┐
  │     System      │ Priority │            Role            │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │                 │          │ Listen to WS               │
  │ RoundSystem     │ 0        │ ROUND_START/ROUND_END,     │
  │                 │          │ flip globals, manage timer │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │                 │          │ Read gamepad inputs, spawn │
  │ WeaponSystem    │ 1        │  projectiles on trigger,   │
  │                 │          │ swing sword                │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │                 │          │ Move enemies (simple       │
  │ EnemyAISystem   │ 2        │ bob/chase) — only when     │
  │                 │          │ round running              │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │                 │          │ Player↔item, sword↔item,   │
  │ CollisionSystem │ 3        │ projectile↔item distance   │
  │                 │          │ checks                     │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ HUDSystem       │ 35       │ Timer + score + health     │
  │                 │          │ panel follows head         │
  └─────────────────┴──────────┴────────────────────────────┘

  Phase 4 — Weapons

  Left = Sword (melee, attached to gripSpaces.left):
  - BoxGeometry(0.03, 0.8, 0.03) hilt-forward, bright blade
  - Squeeze-to-swing (or always active); track tip velocity
  - On tip proximity (< 0.3m) to enemy + velocity > 2 m/s →
  damage

  Right = Gun (ranged, attached to gripSpaces.right):
  - Simple pistol shape (CylinderGeometry)
  - On trigger press: spawn Projectile entity at barrel,
  forward velocity 15 m/s, 3s lifetime
  - Haptic feedback on fire

  Why left/right assignment: left is dominant-handed
  (defensive/reactive), right triggers are easier to pull
  quickly for aimed shots. Can swap if needed.

  Clarification: "Joystick" in your ask — assuming you mean the
   controller itself, not the thumbstick. The thumbstick will
  stay for locomotion (smooth move).

  Phase 5 — Collision (phase-1: distance-based, no physics)

  Simple squared-distance checks per frame, zero-allocation:
  - Player head → each GameItem within 0.5m
  - Sword tip → each enemy within 0.3m
  - Each Projectile → each enemy within 0.2m

  When round starts, VR spawns an entity per grid cell; when
  round ends or item killed, entity.dispose().

  Later: swap to PhysicsBody + PhysicsShape(Sphere) triggers
  once stable — avoids per-frame N×M checks.

  Phase 6 — Feedback

  - Audio: AudioSource per role (goal-collect.mp3,
  enemy-hit.mp3, gun-fire.mp3, round-start.mp3, round-end.mp3)
  - Visual: items pulse/glow when role=goal active; shake/flash
   when enemy hit
  - HUD: screen-space panel — ⏱ 00:28   ⭐ 3/7   ❤ 87
  - Broadcast page: scoreboard per user

  ---
  Implementation order (suggest one PR per phase)

  1. Portal UI — button rename + timer controls + WS round
  messages (no VR changes; portal standalone works)
  2. Role metadata — item schema + palette styling
  3. RoundSystem — globals + WS listener + HUD timer (no
  gameplay yet)
  4. Weapons — sword + gun visible, firing (no hits yet)
  5. Enemies + Collisions — AI + distance collisions + scoring
  6. Polish — audio, haptics, win/lose states, broadcast
  scoreboard

  ---
  Open questions before coding

  1. Win condition: all goals collected, or time-based score?
  Both?
  2. Health/damage: is VR player mortal? Round ends if they
  die?
  3. Shared score: per-user or team total? (The server is
  already multi-user.)
  4. Enemy AI difficulty: static-bob (easy) vs. chase-player
  (intense)?
  5. Pause on start: 3-2-1 countdown before items activate?

❯ /model
  ⎿  Set model to Opus 4.6 (1M context) (default)

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is it necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items from the local
  grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in the portal's
   local state and sends a GRID_PLACE message for each to the
  server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is already
  auto-sent via wsSend({ type: 'GRID_PLACE', ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and broadcasts updates to
  all clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its in-memory
  state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal items were
  already placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button — a safety
  net, not part of the core flow. You could remove it without
  breaking anything.

❯ ok, we don't need that button, we can change the botton fso
  it is now called start round. the way it works, is in the
  center top of the interface there will be a timer. you can
  also set the time for the round at the top. when you press
  start round, it will start the round for the vr contestant.
  when the round starts, some items will come to life, some
  items will be goals to win the round. we will design what
  the emoji's do in terms of items and what not. add collision
   detection with teh user and the emoji, then we can set this
   up to be item collecting, or bad guy collisions later, or
  collecting objectives. i also want you to make the left hand
   joystic a sword, and the right one a gun. come up with this
   plan.

⏺ Let me load IWSDK planning guidance first, then draft the
  plan.

⏺ Skill(iwsdk-planner)
  ⎿  Successfully loaded skill

⏺ Round-Based Game Plan

  Phase 1 — Portal UI (round controls)

  Replace Transmit button + add timer controls in top bar:

  [Clear] [30s ▾] [00:30]← center, monospace] [Start Round]

  - <input type="number"> for duration (default 30s, 5–600)
  - <div id="timer"> — big monospace clock in center
  - <button id="start-round-btn"> — sends ROUND_START with
  duration, then locally runs countdown synced to server

  New WS message types:
  - ROUND_START: { type, duration, startedAt } — broadcast to
  all clients
  - ROUND_END: { type, reason: 'timeout'|'completed' }
  - ROUND_SCORE: { type, userId, points, reason }

  Phase 2 — Item schema & roles

  Extend grid item to include a role field:

  { type, icon, label, role }

  ┌──────────┬──────────┬──────────────────────────────────┐
  │   Role   │  Emojis  │    Behavior when round active    │
  ├──────────┼──────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
  │ goal     │ ⭐ 💎 🎯 │ Collect by touch → score +1,     │
  │          │  🌀      │ despawn, play chime              │
  ├──────────┼──────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
  │ enemy    │ 🔥 🤖 👻 │ Chase/patrol; damage player on   │
  │          │  💀      │ touch; killable with sword/gun   │
  ├──────────┼──────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
  │ pickup   │ ❤️  🍄 ⚡ │ Powerup (heal/speed) — stretch   │
  │          │          │ goal                             │
  ├──────────┼──────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
  │ obstacle │ 🪵 🧱 🪑 │ Static, impassable (later:       │
  │          │          │ physics collision)               │
  ├──────────┼──────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
  │ decor    │ 🌱 🎵 🖥️  │ Neutral, passable, no            │
  │          │  🏮      │ interaction                      │
  └──────────┴──────────┴──────────────────────────────────┘

  Portal palette gets role tags via CSS — goals glow gold,
  enemies red. No UI changes needed to place them; role is
  derived from icon.

  Phase 3 — VR round state (new ECS components/systems)

  New components:
  GameItem { role, health, points, speed, behavior }
  RoundState  // tag on singleton entity
  Weapon { type: 'sword' | 'gun', damage, cooldown }
  Projectile { velocity, lifetime, damage, ownerEntity }
  Health { current, max }

  World globals (shared state):
  world.globals.roundRunning = signal(false)
  world.globals.roundEndsAt = signal(0)
  world.globals.score = signal(0)
  world.globals.playerHealth = signal(100)

  New systems (register in order):

  ┌─────────────────┬──────────┬────────────────────────────┐
  │     System      │ Priority │            Role            │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │                 │          │ Listen to WS               │
  │ RoundSystem     │ 0        │ ROUND_START/ROUND_END,     │
  │                 │          │ flip globals, manage timer │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │                 │          │ Read gamepad inputs, spawn │
  │ WeaponSystem    │ 1        │  projectiles on trigger,   │
  │                 │          │ swing sword                │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │                 │          │ Move enemies (simple       │
  │ EnemyAISystem   │ 2        │ bob/chase) — only when     │
  │                 │          │ round running              │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │                 │          │ Player↔item, sword↔item,   │
  │ CollisionSystem │ 3        │ projectile↔item distance   │
  │                 │          │ checks                     │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ HUDSystem       │ 35       │ Timer + score + health     │
  │                 │          │ panel follows head         │
  └─────────────────┴──────────┴────────────────────────────┘

  Phase 4 — Weapons

  Left = Sword (melee, attached to gripSpaces.left):
  - BoxGeometry(0.03, 0.8, 0.03) hilt-forward, bright blade
  - Squeeze-to-swing (or always active); track tip velocity
  - On tip proximity (< 0.3m) to enemy + velocity > 2 m/s →
  damage

  Right = Gun (ranged, attached to gripSpaces.right):
  - Simple pistol shape (CylinderGeometry)
  - On trigger press: spawn Projectile entity at barrel,
  forward velocity 15 m/s, 3s lifetime
  - Haptic feedback on fire

  Why left/right assignment: left is dominant-handed
  (defensive/reactive), right triggers are easier to pull
  quickly for aimed shots. Can swap if needed.

  Clarification: "Joystick" in your ask — assuming you mean the
   controller itself, not the thumbstick. The thumbstick will
  stay for locomotion (smooth move).

  Phase 5 — Collision (phase-1: distance-based, no physics)

  Simple squared-distance checks per frame, zero-allocation:
  - Player head → each GameItem within 0.5m
  - Sword tip → each enemy within 0.3m
  - Each Projectile → each enemy within 0.2m

  When round starts, VR spawns an entity per grid cell; when
  round ends or item killed, entity.dispose().

  Later: swap to PhysicsBody + PhysicsShape(Sphere) triggers
  once stable — avoids per-frame N×M checks.

  Phase 6 — Feedback

  - Audio: AudioSource per role (goal-collect.mp3,
  enemy-hit.mp3, gun-fire.mp3, round-start.mp3, round-end.mp3)
  - Visual: items pulse/glow when role=goal active; shake/flash
   when enemy hit
  - HUD: screen-space panel — ⏱ 00:28   ⭐ 3/7   ❤ 87
  - Broadcast page: scoreboard per user

  ---
  Implementation order (suggest one PR per phase)

  1. Portal UI — button rename + timer controls + WS round
  messages (no VR changes; portal standalone works)
  2. Role metadata — item schema + palette styling
  3. RoundSystem — globals + WS listener + HUD timer (no
  gameplay yet)
  4. Weapons — sword + gun visible, firing (no hits yet)
  5. Enemies + Collisions — AI + distance collisions + scoring
  6. Polish — audio, haptics, win/lose states, broadcast
  scoreboard

  ---
  Open questions before coding

  1. Win condition: all goals collected, or time-based score?
  Both?
  2. Health/damage: is VR player mortal? Round ends if they
  die?
  3. Shared score: per-user or team total? (The server is
  already multi-user.)
  4. Enemy AI difficulty: static-bob (easy) vs. chase-player
  (intense)?
  5. Pause on start: 3-2-1 countdown before items activate?

  Answer those and I'll start with Phase 1 (Portal UI + WS
  round messages) — that's self-contained and shippable.

✻ Brewed for 1m 14s

❯ /model
  ⎿  Set model to Opus 4.6 (1M context) (default)

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is it necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items from the local
  grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in the portal's
  local state and sends a GRID_PLACE message for each to the
  server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is already auto-sent
  via wsSend({ type: 'GRID_PLACE', ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and broadcasts updates to all
   clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its in-memory state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal items were already
   placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button — a safety net,
   not part of the core flow. You could remove it without breaking
   anything.

❯ ok, we don't need that button, we can change the botton fso it
  is now called start round. the way it works, is in the center
  top of the interface there will be a timer. you can also set
  the time for the round at the top. when you press start round,
  it will start the round for the vr contestant. when the round
  starts, some items will come to life, some items will be goals
  to win the round. we will design what the emoji's do in terms
  of items and what not. add collision detection with teh user
  and the emoji, then we can set this up to be item collecting,
  or bad guy collisions later, or collecting objectives. i also
  want you to make the left hand joystic a sword, and the right
  one a gun. come up with this plan.

⏺ Let me load IWSDK planning guidance first, then draft the plan.

⏺ Skill(iwsdk-planner)
  ⎿  Successfully loaded skill

⏺ Round-Based Game Plan

  Phase 1 — Portal UI (round controls)

  Replace Transmit button + add timer controls in top bar:

  [Clear] [30s ▾] [00:30]← center, monospace] [Start Round]

  - <input type="number"> for duration (default 30s, 5–600)
  - <div id="timer"> — big monospace clock in center
  - <button id="start-round-btn"> — sends ROUND_START with
  duration, then locally runs countdown synced to server

  New WS message types:
  - ROUND_START: { type, duration, startedAt } — broadcast to all
  clients
  - ROUND_END: { type, reason: 'timeout'|'completed' }
  - ROUND_SCORE: { type, userId, points, reason }

  Phase 2 — Item schema & roles

  Extend grid item to include a role field:

  { type, icon, label, role }

  ┌──────────┬──────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┐
  │   Role   │  Emojis  │     Behavior when round active      │
  ├──────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ goal     │ ⭐ 💎 🎯 │ Collect by touch → score +1,        │
  │          │  🌀      │ despawn, play chime                 │
  ├──────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ enemy    │ 🔥 🤖 👻 │ Chase/patrol; damage player on      │
  │          │  💀      │ touch; killable with sword/gun      │
  ├──────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ pickup   │ ❤️  🍄 ⚡ │ Powerup (heal/speed) — stretch goal │
  ├──────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ obstacle │ 🪵 🧱 🪑 │ Static, impassable (later: physics  │
  │          │          │ collision)                          │
  ├──────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ decor    │ 🌱 🎵 🖥️  │ Neutral, passable, no interaction   │
  │          │  🏮      │                                     │
  └──────────┴──────────┴─────────────────────────────────────┘

  Portal palette gets role tags via CSS — goals glow gold, enemies
   red. No UI changes needed to place them; role is derived from
  icon.

  Phase 3 — VR round state (new ECS components/systems)

  New components:
  GameItem { role, health, points, speed, behavior }
  RoundState  // tag on singleton entity
  Weapon { type: 'sword' | 'gun', damage, cooldown }
  Projectile { velocity, lifetime, damage, ownerEntity }
  Health { current, max }

  World globals (shared state):
  world.globals.roundRunning = signal(false)
  world.globals.roundEndsAt = signal(0)
  world.globals.score = signal(0)
  world.globals.playerHealth = signal(100)

  New systems (register in order):

  ┌─────────────────┬──────────┬───────────────────────────────┐
  │     System      │ Priority │             Role              │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │                 │          │ Listen to WS                  │
  │ RoundSystem     │ 0        │ ROUND_START/ROUND_END, flip   │
  │                 │          │ globals, manage timer         │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │                 │          │ Read gamepad inputs, spawn    │
  │ WeaponSystem    │ 1        │ projectiles on trigger, swing │
  │                 │          │  sword                        │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │                 │          │ Move enemies (simple          │
  │ EnemyAISystem   │ 2        │ bob/chase) — only when round  │
  │                 │          │ running                       │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │                 │          │ Player↔item, sword↔item,      │
  │ CollisionSystem │ 3        │ projectile↔item distance      │
  │                 │          │ checks                        │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │ HUDSystem       │ 35       │ Timer + score + health panel  │
  │                 │          │ follows head                  │
  └─────────────────┴──────────┴───────────────────────────────┘

  Phase 4 — Weapons

  Left = Sword (melee, attached to gripSpaces.left):
  - BoxGeometry(0.03, 0.8, 0.03) hilt-forward, bright blade
  - Squeeze-to-swing (or always active); track tip velocity
  - On tip proximity (< 0.3m) to enemy + velocity > 2 m/s → damage

  Right = Gun (ranged, attached to gripSpaces.right):
  - Simple pistol shape (CylinderGeometry)
  - On trigger press: spawn Projectile entity at barrel, forward
  velocity 15 m/s, 3s lifetime
  - Haptic feedback on fire

  Why left/right assignment: left is dominant-handed
  (defensive/reactive), right triggers are easier to pull quickly
  for aimed shots. Can swap if needed.

  Clarification: "Joystick" in your ask — assuming you mean the
  controller itself, not the thumbstick. The thumbstick will stay
  for locomotion (smooth move).

  Phase 5 — Collision (phase-1: distance-based, no physics)

  Simple squared-distance checks per frame, zero-allocation:
  - Player head → each GameItem within 0.5m
  - Sword tip → each enemy within 0.3m
  - Each Projectile → each enemy within 0.2m

  When round starts, VR spawns an entity per grid cell; when round
   ends or item killed, entity.dispose().

  Later: swap to PhysicsBody + PhysicsShape(Sphere) triggers once
  stable — avoids per-frame N×M checks.

  Phase 6 — Feedback

  - Audio: AudioSource per role (goal-collect.mp3, enemy-hit.mp3,
  gun-fire.mp3, round-start.mp3, round-end.mp3)
  - Visual: items pulse/glow when role=goal active; shake/flash
  when enemy hit
  - HUD: screen-space panel — ⏱ 00:28   ⭐ 3/7   ❤ 87
  - Broadcast page: scoreboard per user

  ---
  Implementation order (suggest one PR per phase)

  1. Portal UI — button rename + timer controls + WS round
  messages (no VR changes; portal standalone works)
  2. Role metadata — item schema + palette styling
  3. RoundSystem — globals + WS listener + HUD timer (no gameplay
  yet)
  4. Weapons — sword + gun visible, firing (no hits yet)
  5. Enemies + Collisions — AI + distance collisions + scoring
  6. Polish — audio, haptics, win/lose states, broadcast
  scoreboard

  ---
  Open questions before coding

  1. Win condition: all goals collected, or time-based score?
  Both?
  2. Health/damage: is VR player mortal? Round ends if they die?
  3. Shared score: per-user or team total? (The server is already
  multi-user.)
  4. Enemy AI difficulty: static-bob (easy) vs. chase-player
  (intense)?
  5. Pause on start: 3-2-1 countdown before items activate?

  Answer those and I'll start with Phase 1 (Portal UI + WS round
  messages) — that's self-contained and shippable.

✻ Baked for 16m 9s

❯ /model
  ⎿  Set model to Opus 4.6 (1M context) (default)

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is it necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items from the local
  grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in the portal's
  local state and sends a GRID_PLACE message for each to the
  server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is already auto-sent
  via wsSend({ type: 'GRID_PLACE', ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and broadcasts updates to all
   clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its in-memory state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal items were already
   placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button — a safety net,
   not part of the core flow. You could remove it without breaking
   anything.

❯ ok, we don't need that button, we can change the botton fso it
  is now called start round. the way it works, is in the center
  top of the interface there will be a timer. you can also set
  the time for the round at the top. when you press start round,
  it will start the round for the vr contestant. when the round
  starts, some items will come to life, some items will be goals
  to win the round. we will design what the emoji's do in terms
  of items and what not. add collision detection with teh user
  and the emoji, then we can set this up to be item collecting,
  or bad guy collisions later, or collecting objectives. i also
  want you to make the left hand joystic a sword, and the right
  one a gun. come up with this plan.

⏺ Let me load IWSDK planning guidance first, then draft the plan.

⏺ Skill(iwsdk-planner)
  ⎿  Successfully loaded skill

⏺ Round-Based Game Plan

  Phase 1 — Portal UI (round controls)

  Replace Transmit button + add timer controls in top bar:

  [Clear] [30s ▾] [00:30]← center, monospace] [Start Round]

  - <input type="number"> for duration (default 30s, 5–600)
  - <div id="timer"> — big monospace clock in center
  - <button id="start-round-btn"> — sends ROUND_START with duration, then locally runs
  countdown synced to server

  New WS message types:
  - ROUND_START: { type, duration, startedAt } — broadcast to all clients
  - ROUND_END: { type, reason: 'timeout'|'completed' }
  - ROUND_SCORE: { type, userId, points, reason }

  Phase 2 — Item schema & roles

  Extend grid item to include a role field:

  { type, icon, label, role }

  ┌──────────┬────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │   Role   │   Emojis   │                Behavior when round active                 │
  ├──────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ goal     │ ⭐ 💎 🎯   │ Collect by touch → score +1, despawn, play chime          │
  │          │ 🌀         │                                                           │
  ├──────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ enemy    │ 🔥 🤖 👻   │ Chase/patrol; damage player on touch; killable with       │
  │          │ 💀         │ sword/gun                                                 │
  ├──────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ pickup   │ ❤️  🍄 ⚡   │ Powerup (heal/speed) — stretch goal                       │
  ├──────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ obstacle │ 🪵 🧱 🪑   │ Static, impassable (later: physics collision)             │
  ├──────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ decor    │ 🌱 🎵 🖥️    │ Neutral, passable, no interaction                         │
  │          │ 🏮         │                                                           │
  └──────────┴────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Portal palette gets role tags via CSS — goals glow gold, enemies red. No UI changes
  needed to place them; role is derived from icon.

  Phase 3 — VR round state (new ECS components/systems)

  New components:
  GameItem { role, health, points, speed, behavior }
  RoundState  // tag on singleton entity
  Weapon { type: 'sword' | 'gun', damage, cooldown }
  Projectile { velocity, lifetime, damage, ownerEntity }
  Health { current, max }

  World globals (shared state):
  world.globals.roundRunning = signal(false)
  world.globals.roundEndsAt = signal(0)
  world.globals.score = signal(0)
  world.globals.playerHealth = signal(100)

  New systems (register in order):

  ┌─────────────────┬──────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │     System      │ Priority │                         Role                          │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ RoundSystem     │ 0        │ Listen to WS ROUND_START/ROUND_END, flip globals,     │
  │                 │          │ manage timer                                          │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ WeaponSystem    │ 1        │ Read gamepad inputs, spawn projectiles on trigger,    │
  │                 │          │ swing sword                                           │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ EnemyAISystem   │ 2        │ Move enemies (simple bob/chase) — only when round     │
  │                 │          │ running                                               │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ CollisionSystem │ 3        │ Player↔item, sword↔item, projectile↔item distance     │
  │                 │          │ checks                                                │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ HUDSystem       │ 35       │ Timer + score + health panel follows head             │
  └─────────────────┴──────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Phase 4 — Weapons

  Left = Sword (melee, attached to gripSpaces.left):
  - BoxGeometry(0.03, 0.8, 0.03) hilt-forward, bright blade
  - Squeeze-to-swing (or always active); track tip velocity
  - On tip proximity (< 0.3m) to enemy + velocity > 2 m/s → damage

  Right = Gun (ranged, attached to gripSpaces.right):
  - Simple pistol shape (CylinderGeometry)
  - On trigger press: spawn Projectile entity at barrel, forward velocity 15 m/s, 3s
  lifetime
  - Haptic feedback on fire

  Why left/right assignment: left is dominant-handed (defensive/reactive), right triggers
  are easier to pull quickly for aimed shots. Can swap if needed.

  Clarification: "Joystick" in your ask — assuming you mean the controller itself, not the
   thumbstick. The thumbstick will stay for locomotion (smooth move).

  Phase 5 — Collision (phase-1: distance-based, no physics)

  Simple squared-distance checks per frame, zero-allocation:
  - Player head → each GameItem within 0.5m
  - Sword tip → each enemy within 0.3m
  - Each Projectile → each enemy within 0.2m

  When round starts, VR spawns an entity per grid cell; when round ends or item killed,
  entity.dispose().

  Later: swap to PhysicsBody + PhysicsShape(Sphere) triggers once stable — avoids
  per-frame N×M checks.

  Phase 6 — Feedback

  - Audio: AudioSource per role (goal-collect.mp3, enemy-hit.mp3, gun-fire.mp3,
  round-start.mp3, round-end.mp3)
  - Visual: items pulse/glow when role=goal active; shake/flash when enemy hit
  - HUD: screen-space panel — ⏱ 00:28   ⭐ 3/7   ❤ 87
  - Broadcast page: scoreboard per user

  ---
  Implementation order (suggest one PR per phase)

  1. Portal UI — button rename + timer controls + WS round messages (no VR changes; portal
   standalone works)
  2. Role metadata — item schema + palette styling
  3. RoundSystem — globals + WS listener + HUD timer (no gameplay yet)
  4. Weapons — sword + gun visible, firing (no hits yet)
  5. Enemies + Collisions — AI + distance collisions + scoring
  6. Polish — audio, haptics, win/lose states, broadcast scoreboard

  ---
  Open questions before coding

  1. Win condition: all goals collected, or time-based score? Both?
  2. Health/damage: is VR player mortal? Round ends if they die?
  3. Shared score: per-user or team total? (The server is already multi-user.)
  4. Enemy AI difficulty: static-bob (easy) vs. chase-player (intense)?
  5. Pause on start: 3-2-1 countdown before items activate?

  Answer those and I'll start with Phase 1 (Portal UI + WS round messages) — that's
  self-contained and shippable.

✻ Brewed for 1m 14s

    - /portal.html — grid planner
    - /broadcast.html — broadcast view

  wss://ar-app-ws-production.up.railway.app

  The portal should now show "Connected" instead of
  "Connecting..." when you visit
  https://ar-app-jade.vercel.app/portal.html.

✻ Baked for 16m 9s

❯ /model
  ⎿  Set model to Opus 4.6 (1M context) (default)

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is it necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items from the local
  grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in the portal's
  local state and sends a GRID_PLACE message for each to the
  server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is already auto-sent
  via wsSend({ type: 'GRID_PLACE', ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and broadcasts updates to all
   clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its in-memory state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal items were already
   placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button — a safety net,
   not part of the core flow. You could remove it without breaking
   anything.

❯ ok, we don't need that button, we can change the botton fso it
  is now called start round. the way it works, is in the center
  top of the interface there will be a timer. you can also set
  the time for the round at the top. when you press start round,
  it will start the round for the vr contestant. when the round
  starts, some items will come to life, some items will be goals
  to win the round. we will design what the emoji's do in terms
  of items and what not. add collision detection with teh user
  and the emoji, then we can set this up to be item collecting,
  or bad guy collisions later, or collecting objectives. i also
  want you to make the left hand joystic a sword, and the right
  one a gun. come up with this plan.

⏺ Let me load IWSDK planning guidance first, then draft the plan.

⏺ Round-Based Game Plan

  Phase 1 — Portal UI (round controls)

  Replace Transmit button + add timer controls in top bar:

  [Clear] [30s ▾] [00:30]← center, monospace] [Start Round]

  - <input type="number"> for duration (default 30s, 5–600)
  - <div id="timer"> — big monospace clock in center
  - <button id="start-round-btn"> — sends ROUND_START with duration, then locally runs countdown synced to
   server

  New WS message types:
  - ROUND_START: { type, duration, startedAt } — broadcast to all clients
  - ROUND_END: { type, reason: 'timeout'|'completed' }
  - ROUND_SCORE: { type, userId, points, reason }

  Phase 2 — Item schema & roles

  Extend grid item to include a role field:

  { type, icon, label, role }

  ┌──────────┬─────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │   Role   │   Emojis    │                  Behavior when round active                   │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ goal     │ ⭐ 💎 🎯 🌀 │ Collect by touch → score +1, despawn, play chime              │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ enemy    │ 🔥 🤖 👻 💀 │ Chase/patrol; damage player on touch; killable with sword/gun │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ pickup   │ ❤️  🍄 ⚡    │ Powerup (heal/speed) — stretch goal                           │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ obstacle │ 🪵 🧱 🪑    │ Static, impassable (later: physics collision)                 │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ decor    │ 🌱 🎵 🖥️  🏮 │ Neutral, passable, no interaction                             │
  └──────────┴─────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Portal palette gets role tags via CSS — goals glow gold, enemies red. No UI changes needed to place
  them; role is derived from icon.

  Phase 3 — VR round state (new ECS components/systems)

  New components:
  GameItem { role, health, points, speed, behavior }
  RoundState  // tag on singleton entity
  Weapon { type: 'sword' | 'gun', damage, cooldown }
  Projectile { velocity, lifetime, damage, ownerEntity }
  Health { current, max }

  World globals (shared state):
  world.globals.roundRunning = signal(false)
  world.globals.roundEndsAt = signal(0)
  world.globals.score = signal(0)
  world.globals.playerHealth = signal(100)

  New systems (register in order):

  ┌─────────────────┬──────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │     System      │ Priority │                              Role                              │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ RoundSystem     │ 0        │ Listen to WS ROUND_START/ROUND_END, flip globals, manage timer │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ WeaponSystem    │ 1        │ Read gamepad inputs, spawn projectiles on trigger, swing sword │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ EnemyAISystem   │ 2        │ Move enemies (simple bob/chase) — only when round running      │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ CollisionSystem │ 3        │ Player↔item, sword↔item, projectile↔item distance checks       │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ HUDSystem       │ 35       │ Timer + score + health panel follows head                      │
  └─────────────────┴──────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Phase 4 — Weapons

  Left = Sword (melee, attached to gripSpaces.left):
  - BoxGeometry(0.03, 0.8, 0.03) hilt-forward, bright blade
  - Squeeze-to-swing (or always active); track tip velocity
  - On tip proximity (< 0.3m) to enemy + velocity > 2 m/s → damage

  Right = Gun (ranged, attached to gripSpaces.right):
  - Simple pistol shape (CylinderGeometry)
  - On trigger press: spawn Projectile entity at barrel, forward velocity 15 m/s, 3s lifetime
  - Haptic feedback on fire

  Why left/right assignment: left is dominant-handed (defensive/reactive), right triggers are easier to
  pull quickly for aimed shots. Can swap if needed.

  Clarification: "Joystick" in your ask — assuming you mean the controller itself, not the thumbstick. The
   thumbstick will stay for locomotion (smooth move).

  Phase 5 — Collision (phase-1: distance-based, no physics)

  Simple squared-distance checks per frame, zero-allocation:
  - Player head → each GameItem within 0.5m
  - Sword tip → each enemy within 0.3m
  - Each Projectile → each enemy within 0.2m

  When round starts, VR spawns an entity per grid cell; when round ends or item killed, entity.dispose().

  Later: swap to PhysicsBody + PhysicsShape(Sphere) triggers once stable — avoids per-frame N×M checks.

  Phase 6 — Feedback

  - Audio: AudioSource per role (goal-collect.mp3, enemy-hit.mp3, gun-fire.mp3, round-start.mp3,
  round-end.mp3)
  - Visual: items pulse/glow when role=goal active; shake/flash when enemy hit
  - HUD: screen-space panel — ⏱ 00:28   ⭐ 3/7   ❤ 87
  - Broadcast page: scoreboard per user

  ---
  Implementation order (suggest one PR per phase)

  1. Portal UI — button rename + timer controls + WS round messages (no VR changes; portal standalone
  works)
  2. Role metadata — item schema + palette styling
  3. RoundSystem — globals + WS listener + HUD timer (no gameplay yet)
  4. Weapons — sword + gun visible, firing (no hits yet)
  5. Enemies + Collisions — AI + distance collisions + scoring
  6. Polish — audio, haptics, win/lose states, broadcast scoreboard

  ---
  Open questions before coding

  1. Win condition: all goals collected, or time-based score? Both?
  2. Health/damage: is VR player mortal? Round ends if they die?
  3. Shared score: per-user or team total? (The server is already multi-user.)
  4. Enemy AI difficulty: static-bob (easy) vs. chase-player (intense)?
  5. Pause on start: 3-2-1 countdown before items activate?

  Answer those and I'll start with Phase 1 (Portal UI + WS round messages) — that's self-contained and
  shippable.

❯ /model
  ⎿  Set model to Opus 4.6 (1M context) (default)

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is it necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items from the local
  grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in the portal's
  local state and sends a GRID_PLACE message for each to the
  server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is already auto-sent
  via wsSend({ type: 'GRID_PLACE', ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and broadcasts updates to all
   clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its in-memory state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal items were already
   placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button — a safety net,
   not part of the core flow. You could remove it without breaking
   anything.

❯ ok, we don't need that button, we can change the botton fso it
  is now called start round. the way it works, is in the center
  top of the interface there will be a timer. you can also set
  the time for the round at the top. when you press start round,
  it will start the round for the vr contestant. when the round
  starts, some items will come to life, some items will be goals
  to win the round. we will design what the emoji's do in terms
  of items and what not. add collision detection with teh user
  and the emoji, then we can set this up to be item collecting,
  or bad guy collisions later, or collecting objectives. i also
  want you to make the left hand joystic a sword, and the right
  one a gun. come up with this plan.

⏺ Let me load IWSDK planning guidance first, then draft the plan.

⏺ Skill(iwsdk-planner)

⏺ Round-Based Game Plan

  Phase 1 — Portal UI (round controls)

  Replace Transmit button + add timer controls in top bar:

  [Clear] [30s ▾] [00:30]← center, monospace] [Start Round]

  - <input type="number"> for duration (default 30s, 5–600)
  - <div id="timer"> — big monospace clock in center
  - <button id="start-round-btn"> — sends ROUND_START with duration, then locally runs countdown synced to server

  New WS message types:
  - ROUND_START: { type, duration, startedAt } — broadcast to all clients
  - ROUND_END: { type, reason: 'timeout'|'completed' }
  - ROUND_SCORE: { type, userId, points, reason }

  Phase 2 — Item schema & roles

  Extend grid item to include a role field:

  { type, icon, label, role }


  ┌──────────┬─────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │   Role   │   Emojis    │                  Behavior when round active                   │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ goal     │ ⭐ 💎 🎯 🌀 │ Collect by touch → score +1, despawn, play chime              │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ enemy    │ 🔥 🤖 👻 💀 │ Chase/patrol; damage player on touch; killable with sword/gun │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ pickup   │ ❤️  🍄 ⚡    │ Powerup (heal/speed) — stretch goal                           │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ obstacle │ 🪵 🧱 🪑    │ Static, impassable (later: physics collision)                 │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ decor    │ 🌱 🎵 🖥️  🏮 │ Neutral, passable, no interaction                             │
  └──────────┴─────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Portal palette gets role tags via CSS — goals glow gold, enemies red. No UI changes needed to place them; role is derived from icon.

  Phase 3 — VR round state (new ECS components/systems)

  New components:
  GameItem { role, health, points, speed, behavior }
  RoundState  // tag on singleton entity
  Weapon { type: 'sword' | 'gun', damage, cooldown }
  Projectile { velocity, lifetime, damage, ownerEntity }
  Health { current, max }

  World globals (shared state):
  world.globals.roundRunning = signal(false)
  world.globals.roundEndsAt = signal(0)
  world.globals.score = signal(0)
  world.globals.playerHealth = signal(100)

  New systems (register in order):


  ┌─────────────────┬──────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │     System      │ Priority │                              Role                              │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ RoundSystem     │ 0        │ Listen to WS ROUND_START/ROUND_END, flip globals, manage timer │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ WeaponSystem    │ 1        │ Read gamepad inputs, spawn projectiles on trigger, swing sword │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ EnemyAISystem   │ 2        │ Move enemies (simple bob/chase) — only when round running      │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ CollisionSystem │ 3        │ Player↔item, sword↔item, projectile↔item distance checks       │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ HUDSystem       │ 35       │ Timer + score + health panel follows head                      │
  └─────────────────┴──────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Phase 4 — Weapons

  Left = Sword (melee, attached to gripSpaces.left):
  - BoxGeometry(0.03, 0.8, 0.03) hilt-forward, bright blade
  - Squeeze-to-swing (or always active); track tip velocity
  - On tip proximity (< 0.3m) to enemy + velocity > 2 m/s → damage

  Right = Gun (ranged, attached to gripSpaces.right):
  - Simple pistol shape (CylinderGeometry)
  - On trigger press: spawn Projectile entity at barrel, forward velocity 15 m/s, 3s lifetime
  - Haptic feedback on fire

  Why left/right assignment: left is dominant-handed (defensive/reactive), right triggers are easier to pull quickly for aimed shots. Can swap
   if needed.

  Clarification: "Joystick" in your ask — assuming you mean the controller itself, not the thumbstick. The thumbstick will stay for locomotion
   (smooth move).

  Phase 5 — Collision (phase-1: distance-based, no physics)

  Simple squared-distance checks per frame, zero-allocation:
  - Player head → each GameItem within 0.5m
  - Sword tip → each enemy within 0.3m
  - Each Projectile → each enemy within 0.2m

  When round starts, VR spawns an entity per grid cell; when round ends or item killed, entity.dispose().

  Later: swap to PhysicsBody + PhysicsShape(Sphere) triggers once stable — avoids per-frame N×M checks.

  Phase 6 — Feedback

  - Audio: AudioSource per role (goal-collect.mp3, enemy-hit.mp3, gun-fire.mp3, round-start.mp3, round-end.mp3)
  - Visual: items pulse/glow when role=goal active; shake/flash when enemy hit
  - HUD: screen-space panel — ⏱ 00:28   ⭐ 3/7   ❤ 87
  - Broadcast page: scoreboard per user

  ---
  Implementation order (suggest one PR per phase)

  1. Portal UI — button rename + timer controls + WS round messages (no VR changes; portal standalone works)
  2. Role metadata — item schema + palette styling
  3. RoundSystem — globals + WS listener + HUD timer (no gameplay yet)
  4. Weapons — sword + gun visible, firing (no hits yet)
  5. Enemies + Collisions — AI + distance collisions + scoring
  6. Polish — audio, haptics, win/lose states, broadcast scoreboard

  ---
  Open questions before coding

  1. Win condition: all goals collected, or time-based score? Both?
  2. Health/damage: is VR player mortal? Round ends if they die?
  3. Shared score: per-user or team total? (The server is already multi-user.)
  4. Enemy AI difficulty: static-bob (easy) vs. chase-player (intense)?
  5. Pause on start: 3-2-1 countdown before items activate?

❯ /model
  ⎿  Set model to Opus 4.6 (1M context) (default)

❯ ok, so how does this transmit button work, is it necessary?

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Transmit button — manually re-sends all items from the local
  grid to the WebSocket server.

  How it works: Loops through every placed item in the portal's
  local state and sends a GRID_PLACE message for each to the
  server.

  Is it necessary? Probably not for normal use:
  - Every time you tap/drag a cell, the item is already auto-sent
  via wsSend({ type: 'GRID_PLACE', ... })
  - The server keeps state in memory and broadcasts updates to all
   clients

  When it would matter:
  - If the server restarted/crashed and lost its in-memory state
  - If the VR client (re)connected after portal items were already
   placed
  - Debugging desync issues

  It's a manual "force-push my local state" button — a safety net,
   not part of the core flow. You could remove it without breaking
   anything.

❯ ok, we don't need that button, we can change the botton fso it
  is now called start round. the way it works, is in the center
  top of the interface there will be a timer. you can also set
  the time for the round at the top. when you press start round,
  it will start the round for the vr contestant. when the round
  starts, some items will come to life, some items will be goals
  to win the round. we will design what the emoji's do in terms
  of items and what not. add collision detection with teh user
  and the emoji, then we can set this up to be item collecting,
  or bad guy collisions later, or collecting objectives. i also
  want you to make the left hand joystic a sword, and the right
  one a gun. come up with this plan.

⏺ Let me load IWSDK planning guidance first, then draft the plan.

⏺ Skill(iwsdk-planner)
  ⎿  Successfully loaded skill

⏺ Round-Based Game Plan

  Phase 1 — Portal UI (round controls)

  Replace Transmit button + add timer controls in top bar:

  [Clear] [30s ▾] [00:30]← center, monospace] [Start Round]

  - <input type="number"> for duration (default 30s, 5–600)
  - <div id="timer"> — big monospace clock in center
  - <button id="start-round-btn"> — sends ROUND_START with duration, then locally runs countdown synced to server

  New WS message types:
  - ROUND_START: { type, duration, startedAt } — broadcast to all clients
  - ROUND_END: { type, reason: 'timeout'|'completed' }
  - ROUND_SCORE: { type, userId, points, reason }

  Phase 2 — Item schema & roles

  Extend grid item to include a role field:

  { type, icon, label, role }


  ┌──────────┬─────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │   Role   │   Emojis    │                  Behavior when round active                   │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ goal     │ ⭐ 💎 🎯 🌀 │ Collect by touch → score +1, despawn, play chime              │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ enemy    │ 🔥 🤖 👻 💀 │ Chase/patrol; damage player on touch; killable with sword/gun │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ pickup   │ ❤️  🍄 ⚡    │ Powerup (heal/speed) — stretch goal                           │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ obstacle │ 🪵 🧱 🪑    │ Static, impassable (later: physics collision)                 │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ decor    │ 🌱 🎵 🖥️  🏮 │ Neutral, passable, no interaction                             │
  └──────────┴─────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Portal palette gets role tags via CSS — goals glow gold, enemies red. No UI changes needed to place them; role is derived from icon.

  Phase 3 — VR round state (new ECS components/systems)

  New components:
  GameItem { role, health, points, speed, behavior }
  RoundState  // tag on singleton entity
  Weapon { type: 'sword' | 'gun', damage, cooldown }
  Projectile { velocity, lifetime, damage, ownerEntity }
  Health { current, max }

  World globals (shared state):
  world.globals.roundRunning = signal(false)
  world.globals.roundEndsAt = signal(0)
  world.globals.score = signal(0)
  world.globals.playerHealth = signal(100)

  New systems (register in order):


  ┌─────────────────┬──────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │     System      │ Priority │                              Role                              │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ RoundSystem     │ 0        │ Listen to WS ROUND_START/ROUND_END, flip globals, manage timer │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ WeaponSystem    │ 1        │ Read gamepad inputs, spawn projectiles on trigger, swing sword │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ EnemyAISystem   │ 2        │ Move enemies (simple bob/chase) — only when round running      │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ CollisionSystem │ 3        │ Player↔item, sword↔item, projectile↔item distance checks       │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ HUDSystem       │ 35       │ Timer + score + health panel follows head                      │
  └─────────────────┴──────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Phase 4 — Weapons

  Left = Sword (melee, attached to gripSpaces.left):
  - BoxGeometry(0.03, 0.8, 0.03) hilt-forward, bright blade
  - Squeeze-to-swing (or always active); track tip velocity
  - On tip proximity (< 0.3m) to enemy + velocity > 2 m/s → damage

  Right = Gun (ranged, attached to gripSpaces.right):
  - Simple pistol shape (CylinderGeometry)
  - On trigger press: spawn Projectile entity at barrel, forward velocity 15 m/s, 3s lifetime
  - Haptic feedback on fire

  Why left/right assignment: left is dominant-handed (defensive/reactive), right triggers are easier to pull quickly for aimed shots. Can swap if
  needed.

  Clarification: "Joystick" in your ask — assuming you mean the controller itself, not the thumbstick. The thumbstick will stay for locomotion
  (smooth move).

  Phase 5 — Collision (phase-1: distance-based, no physics)

  Simple squared-distance checks per frame, zero-allocation:
  - Player head → each GameItem within 0.5m
  - Sword tip → each enemy within 0.3m
  - Each Projectile → each enemy within 0.2m

  When round starts, VR spawns an entity per grid cell; when round ends or item killed, entity.dispose().

  Later: swap to PhysicsBody + PhysicsShape(Sphere) triggers once stable — avoids per-frame N×M checks.

  Phase 6 — Feedback

  - Audio: AudioSource per role (goal-collect.mp3, enemy-hit.mp3, gun-fire.mp3, round-start.mp3, round-end.mp3)
  - Visual: items pulse/glow when role=goal active; shake/flash when enemy hit
  - HUD: screen-space panel — ⏱ 00:28   ⭐ 3/7   ❤ 87
  - Broadcast page: scoreboard per user

  ---
  Implementation order (suggest one PR per phase)

  1. Portal UI — button rename + timer controls + WS round messages (no VR changes; portal standalone works)
  2. Role metadata — item schema + palette styling
  3. RoundSystem — globals + WS listener + HUD timer (no gameplay yet)
  4. Weapons — sword + gun visible, firing (no hits yet)
  5. Enemies + Collisions — AI + distance collisions + scoring
  6. Polish — audio, haptics, win/lose states, broadcast scoreboard

  ---
  Open questions before coding

  1. Win condition: all goals collected, or time-based score? Both?
  2. Health/damage: is VR player mortal? Round ends if they die?
  3. Shared score: per-user or team total? (The server is already multi-user.)
  4. Enemy AI difficulty: static-bob (easy) vs. chase-player (intense)?
  5. Pause on start: 3-2-1 countdown before items activate?

✻ Brewed for 1m 14sI'll start with Phase 1 (Portal UI + WS round messages) — that's self-contained and shippable.
